sections.text: The ballad is introduced with a series of images that prefigure themes in the narrative to come\: action at a distance, the bickering of women, and the multifarious connections between people and things (lines 1-4.5). His wife's sharp rebuke for not being a sufficient provider motivates husband Kajango to go on a journey of trade (lines 5-13.2).
He travels a route marked by named landmarks and villages (lines 14-40).
The singer interrupts the journey by revisiting the ballad's initial poetic images, adding one about passion (lines 42-42.4).

At sunset, Kajango seeks a place to sleep, convincing an elder woman to give him lodging by indicating the similarity of his situation to that of her son who also is on a journey of trade. Kajango swears a blood brotherhood with the woman's neighbor, which creates a relationship of mutual aid (lines 52-64).

Continuing on his journey, Kajango comes upon a rhinoceros and bravely kills it (lines 65-83.1). Immediately beset by a band of local hunters, he proves his entitlement to the spoils of the hunt, removes the rhino's tail and horns, and sets out for home (lines 84-93).

Joyously announcing his presence along the way, he is greeted by his wife in the forecourt of their house (lines 93.1-97.1). But she does not know the value of rhinoceros horns, and she sharply berates him again, this time for bringing worthless things (lines 98-110.2).

Kajango angrily sets out again and travels north toward Ankole (lines 110.3-140). He crosses the Kagera River and asks directions (lines 141-146). He meets royal herders who, seeing what he has brought in trade, convey Kajango to the king (146.1-151.1).

The king trades more than a hundred cattle and a herd of goats for the rhino horns and tail, and Kajango brings them home (lines 152-157.1). He enters triumphantly, announcing his presence with hunting horn and self-praise poetry (lines 157.2-163).
Kajango's wife hurries to receive him. She squeezes some banana mash for him and cooks him a meal, but he refuses, declaring his fatigue (lines 164-172.1).
She makes elaborate herbal preparations for their lovemaking but finds Kajango already asleep (lines 173-81.1). She pleads with and praises him, but he does not wish to be disturbed (lines 182-190).

In the morning Kajango falls into a swoon as though dead (lines 190.1-193.1). The couple's child runs to the fields where the wife has gone to work and calls to the mother, telling her of Kajango's dire condition, and cursing her by invoking named spirits; each spirit is praised with its characteristic praise. The bard elaborates this praise by asserting that the same spirit is known by different names in different kingdoms (lines 194-221). The mother apparently curses her child with a single line (line 222).

With her hoe held in a way to indicate distress, the wife returns from the field and goes to investigate, finding her husband in a dangerous state (lines 223- 238). She strengthens her resolve (lines 239-40.3) and prepares to visit spirit mediums for consultations by gathering cowry-shell currency and twigs useful for divining (lines 241-52).

The wife consults a series of spirits through their human mediums. Each is identified with its characteristic praise epithets. None are successful in divining the cause of the husband's state (lines 253-86).

On her way home, the wife bemoans her condition aloud (lines 287-95). An old woman overhears her; she questions the wife about her loss (lines 296-305) and recommends a person for her to see (lines 306-310).

The wife travels as advised (lines 311-318.1), and out in the open fields she comes upon the agricultural spirits Nyakalembe and Ilimamabuye singing women's songs (lines 319-28.1). Nyakalembe questions the wife, praising her virtue and that of love (lines 328-42.1).
The spirit instructs the wife to visit the high god Wamara (lines 344-52.3) and warns her not to fear what she sees at his palace compound (lines 352.4-382).
The wife goes to Wamara's palace compound and finds what she was warned about (lines 383-419).

Kajango's wife meets the high god Wamara, who questions her and gives her a task to perform (lines 419.1-430). She bravely does as bidden (lines 431-37) and answers further questions (lines 438-40.4).

Wamara declares that her husband will recover and gives her instructions about what will cure him (lines 441-59). At home Kajango begins to show signs of life as his sisters celebrate in song (460-67.1).
The wife sets out from Wamara's palace and travels toward home, gathering substances as Wamara advised her (lines 468-77.2).
Kajango and his wife meet (lines 478-83), argue about the events that occurred (lines 484-518.1), and are reconciled (lines 519-22.1)}}]]</text</document
